cheatsheet:
Structs (Value Types)
- Definition: struct Name { var property: Type }
- Initialization: let instance = Name(property: value) (memberwise init)
- Access: Use dot notation: instance.property
- Mutability: Use 'var' for instances you want to modify
- Copying: Assigning a struct to a new variable creates a full, independent copy
- Methods: Methods that modify properties must be marked 'mutating'
description: Define a struct and create an instance
hints: Model the item as a value type with a single stored property.
Use dot syntax to read the property for output.
Structs get a free 'memberwise' initializer, so you don't need to write 'init' manually here.
lesson:
Lesson: Structs
Structs are the building blocks of most Swift programs. They are 'value types', meaning they are copied when passed around.

Core Concepts:
1. Definition: Use the 'struct' keyword. Properties are variables or constants defined inside.
2. Memberwise Initializer: Swift automatically provides an initializer based on your properties.
3. Value Semantics: When you assign a struct to a new variable, you get a copy. Changes to the copy don't affect the original.
4. Mutability: If you want to change a struct's properties, the instance itself must be declared with 'var'.

Example:
    struct ForgeItem {
        var name: String
        var weight: Int
    }
    var item = ForgeItem(name: "Iron", weight: 5)
    item.weight = 10 // Only works because 'item' is var

Best Practices:
- Use structs for simple data models.
- Favor immutability (let) whenever possible.
title: Struct Basics

hints: Stored properties can be updated on a var instance.
Access the 'heat' property using dot notation: furnace.heat = 1500.
Make sure to print the value after you update it.

title: Methods + self

cheatsheet:
Structs (Value Types)
- Definition: struct Name { var property: Type }
- Initialization: let instance = Name(property: value) (memberwise init)
- Access: Use dot notation: instance.property
- Mutability: Use 'var' for instances you want to modify
- Copying: Assigning a struct to a new variable creates a full, independent copy
- Methods: Methods that modify properties must be marked 'mutating'
hints: Custom initializers can set default values.
Assign 'self.metal = metal' and 'self.weight = 1' inside your init block.
The parameter name in 'init' usually matches the property name, so use 'self' to distinguish them.
lesson:
Lesson: Custom Initializers
Custom initializers allow you to control how a 'struct' instance is created, providing flexibility beyond the default memberwise initializer.

Overview:
1. Define one or more 'init(...)' methods to set up values at creation.
2. Inside the initializer, use 'self.property = value' to assign initial state.
3. Once you define a custom initializer, Swift no longer provides the automatic memberwise one.

Example:
    struct Tool {
        var type: String
        var durability: Int
        init(type: String) {
            self.type = type
            self.durability = 100 // default value
        }
    }

Guidelines:
- Use custom initializers for validation or to provide default values for some properties.
- Keep them simple; their primary job is to ensure the instance is fully and correctly initialized.
title: Custom Init

cheatsheet:
Structs (Value Types)
- Definition: struct Name { var property: Type }
- Initialization: let instance = Name(property: value) (memberwise init)

- Copying: Assigning a struct to a new variable creates a full, independent copy
- Methods: Methods that modify properties must be marked 'mutating'
description: Use mutating to update struct state
hints: Use mutating when a struct method changes properties.
Methods in a struct cannot change properties unless they are marked with the 'mutating' keyword.
The 'mutating' keyword goes before the 'func' keyword.

This is what we mean by 'Value Semantics' – the value itself is passed, not a reference to it.

title: Value Semantics

cheatsheet:
Classes (Reference Types)
- Definition: class Name { var property: Type; init(...) { ... } }
- Reference Semantics: Multiple variables can point to the same instance
- Identity: Use === to check if two variables point to the same instance
- Initializers: Unlike structs, classes DO NOT get a memberwise initializer automatically
- Deinit: A deinitializer runs when the last reference to an instance is released
- Inheritance: Classes can inherit from other classes (subclassing)
hints: Classes need an initializer if they have stored properties.
The 'init' method sets up the initial state of the object.
Create an instance using 'Anvil(weight: 10)'.
lesson:
Lesson: Classes
Classes are 'reference types'. When you assign a class instance to a new variable, both variables point to the same object in memory.

Key Differences from Structs:
1. Reference Semantics: Changing a property through one reference affects all other references to that same instance.
2. Mandatory Initializers: You must provide an 'init' method if your properties don't have default values.
3. Identity vs. Equality: Use '==' for value equality and '===' for reference identity.
4. Lifetime: Classes use Automatic Reference Counting (ARC) to determine when they should be destroyed.

Example:
    class Bellows {
        var power: Int
        init(power: Int) { self.power = power }
    }
    let p1 = Bellows(power: 1)
    let p2 = p1
    p2.power = 10 // p1.power is now also 10

When to Use:
- Use classes when you need shared mutable state or identity.
- Use classes for inheritance or when you need to perform cleanup on deinit.
title: Class Basics

cheatsheet:
Classes (Reference Types)
- Definition: class Name { var property: Type; init(...) { ... } }

- Deinit: A deinitializer runs when the last reference to an instance is released
- Inheritance: Classes can inherit from other classes (subclassing)
hints: Changing a reference affects all references to the same instance.
Since 'primary' and 'secondary' point to the same object, 'primary.power' will reflect the change made via 'secondary'.
Notice that 'primary' is declared with 'let', but you can still change its properties because it's a class.

Declare 'deinit { ... }' inside the class body.
Setting an optional reference to 'nil' releases the instance if it was the only reference.
To create an optional Torch, use the syntax: 'var torch: Torch? = Torch(id: 1)'.

cheatsheet:
Classes (Reference Types)
- Definition: class Name { var property: Type; init(...) { ... } }

hints: Use a struct for independent values and a class for shared state.
The 'Tag' should be a struct because it represents a simple value.
The 'ForgeController' should be a class because it might be shared across the system.

title: Struct vs Class

cheatsheet:
Properties
- Stored Properties: Hold values directly.
- Computed Properties: Do not store a value; instead, they provide a getter and an optional setter to calculate other properties indirectly.
- Property Observers: 'willSet' (runs before) and 'didSet' (runs after) a property changes.
- Lazy Properties: Initialized only when first accessed; must be 'var'.
- Static Properties: Shared across all instances of the type.
hints: Computed properties can translate values in get/set.
The 'get' block should return 'force * 2'.
The 'set' block should update 'force' using 'newValue / 2'.
title: Computed Property
